figures.caption: Figure 3: (Left) Sample Efficiency Comparison. We compare the training curves for Bi-NAC, GRPO, and SCoRe [14], and show that Bi-NAC exhibits superior sample-efficiency, faster convergence speed, and higher final accuracy than the baselines. (Right) State-of-the-Art Comparison Across Scales. We compare Bi-NAC against strong baselines including Behavioral Cloning (BC), Hierarchical-NFT, ArCHer, and SCoRe across three model scales (1B, 3B, and 8B). Bi-NAC consistently outperforms all baselines by significant margins across all benchmarks and scales, demonstrating that our bilevel formulation effectively scales and enhances reasoning capabilities beyond standard reasoning approaches.
Table 6: Training efficiency and inference performance comparison of different methods on the mathematical reasoning task (1B model, rollout size 128, 70 iterations).

Figure 5: (Left) Feedback actionability scores across training iterations. Bi-NAC demonstrates superior feedback quality compared to Hier-FT and SCoRe, achieving a 2.1× improvement in FO score after 1600 steps. (Right) We comparing two Bi-NAC variants: Bi-NAC (2B) which uses two models vs. Bi-NAC (1B) which uses a single model. The single-model (1B) variant achieves performance comparable to the two-model (2B) variant.
Table 7: Hyperparameters used for Bi-NAC experiments with LLAMA-1B and LLAMA-3B backbones.

Figure 4: (Left) Comparison against Feedback Baselines. Bi-NAC significantly outperforms methods that rely on fixed or auxiliary critics on MATH-500, demonstrating the efficacy of our bilevel framework. (Right) Bi-NAC versus GRPO performance comparison across scales. Bi-NAC with 2B parameters outperforms GRPO-trained models with 3B parameters, while Bi-NAC with 6B parameters exceeds those with 7B parameters, demonstrating superior parameter efficiency and overall performance.
Table 1: AIME Benchmark Results (Qwen3-1.7B). Turn 1 is identical across methods by construction. Turn 2 represents one critic-guided refinement step.

Figure 2: Our Bilevel Framework. Top Left: In prior approaches where actor and critic are not aligned (e.g., auxiliary critics or fixed feedback critics), this misalignment often leads to ineffective guidance, where the feedback is either incorrect or ignored by the actor, thus resulting in incorrect final outputs. Bottom Left: We show this in an illustrative example where the feedback and final output are incorrect. Top Right: Bi-NAC explicitly models the dependency between feedback generation and policy improvement by training the critic to maximize the final task performance of the actor, thus also enabling the actor to effectively leverage it. Bottom Right: The illustrative example shows how correct aligned feedback successfully guides the actor from a flawed initial response to the correct final solution.
